episodes.title: Cut to the Chase! Cooler inflation and Warsh
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Standard Chartered's CIO of Fixed Income and FX reviews the June US CPI report and new Fed Chair Warsh's first semi-annual Congressional testimony, both released overnight on July 15th. The combination points to peak inflation and peak Fed hawkishness, a call with direct implications for EM carry trades and rate-hold expectations into 2026.

**Key points**
- Headline CPI fell 0.4% month-on-month, the first monthly decline in consumer prices in years, driven by falling gasoline prices; Core CPI was unchanged.
- Annual headline and core inflation slowed to 3.5% and 2.6% respectively, both below market expectations.
- Shelter rose only 0.1%, the smallest increase since January 2021; Core Goods fell 0.1%, suggesting tariff pass-through is moderating.
- US equities rose and bond yields fell on the print, with short-end Treasury yields falling more than long-end, implying markets are pricing a less hawkish Fed.
- Long-end term premium stayed elevated on lingering fiscal concerns.
- Fed Chair Warsh stuck to his prepared script, reaffirmed price-stability commitment, but notably also referenced full employment (absent from his June press conference); he called the Fed's balance sheet too large post-GFC and confirmed a task force is reviewing balance-sheet policy, with findings due only at year-end.

**FX / rates implications**
- A soft CPI print plus a slow-moving balance-sheet review supports a Fed-on-hold call through 2026, a supportive backdrop for EM carry (INR, IDR, MXN, BRL, COP, PEN) via lower US front-end yields and steady-to-softer DXY.
- Removal of forward guidance and Warsh's balance-sheet-shrinkage inclination are flagged as fresh sources of volatility, warranting diversification and hedging discipline across EM books.
- Elevated long-end term premium on fiscal concerns keeps the UST curve steep, a factor for EM local-rates duration positioning.

**Watch list**
- Middle East conflict escalation as a wildcard risk to the soft-inflation, low-volatility thesis.
- Fed balance-sheet task force, work starting after summer with findings due at year-end.
- Incoming US inflation and Fed commentary for confirmation of the peak-hawkishness thesis.

episodes.title: Getting to Know Central and Eastern Europe is the Key to Unlocking Opportunities
episodes.feed: TMI TreasuryCast (HSBC Treasury Beyond Borders inside)
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
An ING treasury executive discusses Central and Eastern Europe (CEE) as a rising strategic hub for European production and growth, citing stronger GDP growth than Western Europe. The content is corporate-treasury/banking-services focused rather than tradable-market news, but the growth differential and FDI data offer background context on regional economic momentum.

**Key points**
- CEE (defined as Austria through Turkey and Ukraine) is expected to grow 2.5-2.8% through the remainder of 2026, versus 0.8% for Western Europe.
- Poland, Czech Republic and Slovakia show consistent FDI inflows of 2-4% of GDP.
- Near-shoring in automotive, manufacturing and logistics, plus defence, energy and infrastructure investment, is reinforcing CEE's role as a production and services hub (SSCs, BPO, R&D).
- ING highlights growing corporate treasury demand for cross-border visibility, liquidity centralisation and payments coordination across CEE markets.

**FX / rates implications**
- No direct FX or rates trade discussed; the region's currencies (PLN, CZK, HUF, RON) sit outside the trader's core EM Asia/LATAM book, but sustained FDI inflows and near-shoring-driven growth differentials versus the Eurozone are a structural support for CEE currencies and could be a read-across theme for broader EM growth/nearshoring narratives (relevant comparison point for EM Asia manufacturing hubs competing for the same FDI flows).
- Faster CEE growth versus a sluggish 0.8% Western Europe/Eurozone print reinforces a broader theme of EU growth divergence, indirectly relevant to EUR positioning discussions.

**Watch list**
- Continued FDI inflow data for Poland, Czech Republic, Slovakia as a gauge of near-shoring momentum.
- Eurozone growth trajectory (0.8% cited for Western Europe in 2026) versus CEE outperformance.

episodes.title: Kevin Warsh Testifies, IBM’s Warning, and the Incredible Shrinking AI Model 7/14/26
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh gave his first Humphrey-Hawkins testimony to Congress the same morning a cooler-than-expected CPI print hit, with super-core inflation running below 1% annualized over three months. The combination reshuffled Fed pricing and rippled through equities, while a separate IBM software warning and blowout bank earnings (Goldman, BofA, JPMorgan) dominated the tape.

**Key points**
- CPI came in soft; super-core CPI now only 2% y/y, but PCE inflation remains oddly elevated versus CPI, a divergence panelists expect to converge.
- Warsh pledged Fed independence from Trump, said inflation fight is "not mission accomplished," favors shrinking the balance sheet toward T-bills but keeping an ample-reserves regime, and avoided giving explicit rate guidance.
- Fed-cut odds for September fell to ~61% from ~72% pre-CPI/pre-testimony, unwinding some of the hawkish repricing from Fed governor Waller's prior comments.
- Trump backed off a threatened 20% tariff/fee tied to Strait of Hormuz-related oil moves; Brent hit $85/bbl overnight before stabilizing near $78 amid Iran-related explosion reports, with Trump saying the Strait remains open to all shipping except Iran.
- Goldman Sachs, Bank of America and JPMorgan posted record/blowout Q2 results (Goldman equities revenue +72%, IB fees +55%); JPMorgan's Dimon flagged AI cutting up to 40% of headcount in some roles.
- IBM fell ~25%, its worst day since 1987, on a software/mainframe miss as AI budget reallocation and "AI paralysis" hit legacy tech spending.

**FX / rates implications**
- Softer US CPI plus a still-cautious Fed chair modestly dents near-term Fed-cut odds initially priced up on hawkish Waller comments; net effect is USD/UST direction stays data-dependent into the next FOMC, a swing factor for EM carry trades and USD-funded EM positions.
- Rising Brent/WTI on Middle East tension is a two-way risk for EM: negative terms-of-trade for oil importers (India, Philippines, Thailand) and positive for exporters (Colombia, Mexico via Pemex-linked flows).
- Broad risk-on tone (record bank earnings, S&P near highs) supports EM risk appetite and carry, though tech/software wobble (IBM) is a reminder of narrow leadership risk that could spill into broader risk-off if it widens.

**Watch list**
- Warsh's day-two Senate testimony tomorrow for any rate-path signal.
- Next FOMC meeting for whether task-force review and balance-sheet composition shift (toward T-bills) gets formalized.
- Brent/WTI trajectory around Iran/Strait of Hormuz headlines.
- PCE inflation print for confirmation of convergence toward CPI trend.

episodes.title: The New Security Landscape Taking Shape in Asia
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
This episode is a geopolitical/security discussion on the South China Sea dispute's 10th anniversary of the Hague tribunal ruling, a Chinese submarine-launched missile test that alarmed Pacific Island nations, and Japan's rapid remilitarization under PM Takaichi. While primarily a security-affairs podcast rather than a markets one, it carries meaningful geopolitical risk implications for EM Asia FX, particularly PHP, VND, TWD and JPY, given rising China-Philippines and China-Japan tensions.

**Key points**
- 10th anniversary of the 2016 UNCLOS tribunal ruling against China's nine-dash line claim (covering ~85% of South China Sea) reignited diplomatic clashes between China, Philippines, and 14 co-signatory countries including the US.
- Scarborough Shoal standoff intensity has risen dramatically, with a Chinese physical installation placed there earlier this year.
- China test-launched a JL-2/JL-3 submarine-based ICBM (~7,000km range) into the South Pacific with only hours of notice, angering Pacific Island states just as Australia-Fiji signed a new security pact and Modi visited New Zealand.
- China cut rare earth exports to Japan by 90%, partly in response to PM Takaichi's Taiwan-related comments; Japan is pursuing a new intelligence service and defense budget increases.
- ASEAN Code of Conduct on South China Sea unlikely to conclude by Philippines' chairmanship deadline (Dec 31, 2026) given Scarborough tensions and Myanmar distractions.

**FX / rates implications**
- Escalating China-Philippines and China-Japan tensions add a geopolitical risk premium that could weigh on PHP and JPY sentiment if incidents escalate toward military confrontation.
- China's rare earth export cuts to Japan signal willingness to use trade/economic coercion, a risk-off signal relevant to broader China-adjacent EM Asia trade and supply chains (TWD, KRW exposure).
- Persistent South China Sea friction and Taiwan-linkage rhetoric keep a low but non-zero tail risk premium on TWD and regional risk sentiment.

**Watch list**
- ASEAN Code of Conduct deadline, Dec 31, 2026 (Philippines chairmanship) — expected to miss.
- Pacific Islands Forum's forthcoming collective statement on China's missile test.
- Further Japan-China rare earth/trade retaliation following Takaichi's Taiwan comments.
- Scarborough Shoal standoff escalation risk.

episodes.title: Everything is awesome … for banks
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
US Q2 bank earnings season kicked off with blowout results from JPMorgan, Bank of America, Citigroup, Wells Fargo and Goldman Sachs, driven overwhelmingly by AI/tech-linked activity: equity trading, IPO fees (SpaceX), M&A advisory and consumer card spending. For a macro trader the episode is a read on how concentrated and tech-dependent US financial-sector strength has become, and a reminder that a benign US CPI print this morning reinforced the soft-landing narrative.

**Key points**
- Equity trading revenue surged: JPMorgan +86%, Bank of America +70%, Goldman Sachs +72%, even Citigroup (weak in equities) +45%.
- Bank of America revenue growing ~15% y/y, comparable to Microsoft's growth rate.
- Card volumes up double digits/near-double-digits at JPMorgan, BofA, Citi; credit costs (provisions for bad debt) fell at BofA and JPMorgan — no sign of credit quality deterioration.
- Net interest income up double digits across the majors.
- Jamie Dimon estimated total 2025 US capex at ~$4tn, with ~$1tn (a quarter) tied to AI — comparable in size to the entire US defense budget.
- Bank stocks barely moved despite the beats because valuations (Goldman/Morgan Stanley near 3x book value) already priced in strong results; regulatory tone under the current administration/Fed has also turned friendlier to banks.
- Counterpoint: IBM shares fell on results, suggesting enterprise customers are delaying software spend to prioritize AI data-center and chip capex — a signal on timing/duration risk in the tech capex cycle.

**FX / rates implications**
- Benign US CPI this morning supports the soft-landing/disinflation narrative underpinning current Fed pricing — a mild USD/UST tailwind for EM carry if sustained, though no specific inflation figures were given.
- Continued outsized AI-driven capex (~$1tn, a quarter of total US capex) is a key pillar propping up US growth expectations; any AI-bubble unwind (as flagged by IBM's delayed spending) would reset US GDP expectations and likely trigger broad risk-off, pressuring EM FX and widening credit spreads.
- Healthy US consumer spending (card volumes) and low credit costs argue against near-term US recession risk, a background positive for global risk sentiment and EM carry trades.

**Watch list**
- Follow-through Q2 earnings from European banks, where AI exposure is much smaller — a comparison point for the AI-driven US bank outperformance thesis.
- IBM's guidance on delayed enterprise software spending as an early indicator of AI capex cycle timing risk.
- Further US CPI/inflation data and Fed commentary (e.g., Fed's Kevin Warsh remarks referenced) for confirmation of the benign inflation/growth mix.
